Primary & Chronic Headache Types

Type "Buzzword" picture Quick criteria First‑pass management
Migraine without aura Throbbing, light/noise sensitive ≥5 attacks.
Headache 2–72 h in children.
≥2 of: unilateral or bilateral location; pulsating quality; moderate–severe intensity; aggravated by or causing avoidance of routine activity.
≥1 of: nausea and/or vomiting; photophobia and phonophobia together.
Not better accounted for by another diagnosis.		 NSAID or acetaminophen ± antiemetic.
Triptan if age‑appropriate and no contraindication.
Limit acute meds to ≤2–3 days per week.
Tension‑type headache Band‑like, tight, "stress" ≥10 episodes.
Duration 30 min–7 days.
≥2 of: bilateral location; pressing/tightening (non‑pulsating); mild–moderate intensity; not aggravated by routine physical activity.
No nausea or vomiting.
No more than one of photophobia or phonophobia.
Not better explained by another diagnosis.		 Lifestyle optimization (sleep, hydration, stress).
Simple analgesics PRN.
Consider preventive if frequent/chronic.
Migraine with brainstem aura Aura + vertigo/ataxia ≥2 attacks.
Aura with fully reversible visual, sensory, and/or speech/language symptoms.
No motor or retinal aura symptoms.
≥2 brainstem symptoms: dysarthria; vertigo; tinnitus; hypacusis; diplopia; ataxia; decreased level of consciousness.
≥2 of: at least one aura symptom develops over ≥5 min and/or different aura symptoms occur in succession over ≥5 min; each aura symptom lasts 5–60 min (non‑motor); aura is accompanied or followed within 60 min by headache.		 First presentation: treat as possible stroke; obtain MRI/vascular imaging.
Avoid triptans and DHE in classic brainstem aura.
Use non‑vasoconstrictive acute therapy and consider preventive.
Abdominal migraine Episodic belly migraine ≥5 attacks.
Abdominal pain in midline, periumbilical, or poorly localized.
Dull or "just sore" quality.
Moderate or severe intensity.
During attacks, ≥2 of: anorexia; nausea; vomiting; pallor.
Duration 2–72 h if untreated or unsuccessfully treated.
Complete freedom from symptoms between attacks.
GI work‑up negative and no better diagnosis.		 Manage like migraine: trigger/sleep hygiene; reassurance.
Consider prophylaxis (e.g., cyproheptadine or standard migraine preventives depending on age/comorbidities) if frequent/disabling.
Chronic migraine / chronic TTH ≥15 days per month Headache on ≥15 days per month.
Duration >3 months.
On most days, headache has features of migraine and/or tension‑type headache.
Not better explained by another diagnosis.		 Screen for medication overuse, mood, sleep.
Stop overused acute meds; restrict to ≤2–3 days/week.
Start/adjust preventive therapy (e.g., topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol, CGRP‑targeted agents per age/availability).
Medication‑overuse headache "Every day, lots of meds" Headache on ≥15 days per month.
Regular overuse of acute medications (e.g., simple analgesics, NSAIDs, caffeine combos, triptans, some prescriptions).
Headache developed or markedly worsened during overuse.
Headache improves after withdrawal of overused medications.		 Complete withdrawal of acute meds for 2–3 weeks (with bridge meds as needed per protocol).
Then limit acute meds to ≤2–3 days/week.
Initiate or optimize preventive therapy.
Hemiplegic migraine Migraine + reversible weakness ≥2 attacks.
Aura includes fully reversible motor weakness.
Aura also includes fully reversible visual, sensory, and/or speech/language symptoms.
≥2 of: at least one aura symptom develops gradually over ≥5 min and/or different aura symptoms occur in succession over ≥5 min; each non‑motor aura symptom lasts 5–60 min; motor aura symptoms last <72 h; aura is accompanied or followed within 60 min by headache.
Not better explained by another diagnosis.		 First episode: manage as stroke until excluded (MRI, vascular imaging).
Avoid vasoconstrictors (triptans, DHE).
Use non‑vasoconstrictive acute therapy; consider preventive.
Familial vs sporadic determined by family history.
New daily persistent headache (NDPH) "Remember the day it started" Distinct and clearly remembered onset.
Headache becomes daily and unremitting within 24 h of onset.
Persists for >3 months.
≥2 of: bilateral; pressing/tightening (non‑pulsating); mild or moderate intensity; not aggravated by routine physical activity.
Both: no more than one of photophobia, phonophobia, or mild nausea; neither moderate/severe nausea nor vomiting.
Not better accounted for by another diagnosis.		 Diagnostic work‑up to exclude secondary causes (MRI/MRV with contrast, LP with opening pressure and CSF studies, labs as indicated).
Then treat along chronic migraine/TTH lines.
Consider steroids, neuromodulators (e.g., gabapentin/pregabalin, TCAs), nerve blocks, and Botox in refractory cases.
Hemicrania continua Indomethacin‑sensitive, side‑locked Headache present for >3 months.
Unilateral pain without side shift.
Daily and continuous, with no pain‑free periods.
Moderate baseline pain with severe exacerbations.
During exacerbations, at least one ipsilateral autonomic feature: conjunctival injection and/or lacrimation; nasal congestion and/or rhinorrhea; ptosis and/or miosis; or sense of restlessness/agitation.
Complete response to therapeutic doses of indomethacin.		 Perform indomethacin trial (weight‑based; titrate from low dose up to typical max with GI protection).
If responsive, continue at lowest effective dose.
If no response at adequate dose/duration, reconsider diagnosis.

Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension (IIH)

IIH: Clinical and Diagnostic Features

Aspect Key points
Symptoms Headache worse when lying flat.
Headache worse with coughing, straining, or Valsalva.
Transient visual obscurations ("grey‑outs," seconds).
Pulsatile tinnitus.
Examination Papilledema.
Enlarged blind spot on visual fields.
Possible sixth nerve palsy.
Otherwise normal neurologic exam.
CSF LP opening pressure >28 cm H₂O in children.
Normal CSF composition (mildly low protein acceptable).
Imaging MRI brain: no mass, no hydrocephalus, no structural lesion.
MRV: no cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.
Associated factors Obesity.
Rapid weight gain.
Female sex (especially post‑pubertal).
Obstructive sleep apnea.
Vitamin A excess (e.g., isotretinoin).
Tetracyclines (minocycline, doxycycline).
Oral contraceptives.
Growth hormone therapy.
Thyroid disease/treatment.
Corticosteroid withdrawal.

IIH: Treatment Snapshot

Step Key points
First‑line Acetazolamide 25 mg/kg/day in 2–3 divided doses.
Max 100 mg/kg/day or 2,000 mg/day (whichever is lower).
Treat ~3–9 months, until headache and papilledema resolve.
Monitor bicarbonate, electrolytes, paresthesias, GI symptoms.
Adjunctive Weight loss and exercise counseling.
Screen and treat OSA (sleep study, CPAP/ENT).
Consider topiramate if comorbid migraine and/or obesity.
Escalation (vision risk) Short course IV methylprednisolone in acute vision decline while arranging procedures.
Optic nerve sheath fenestration.
CSF shunting (e.g., lumboperitoneal or ventriculoperitoneal shunt) in collaboration with neurosurgery and neuro‑ophthalmology.

Headache Red Flags (Consider Imaging)

Focal neurologic deficit

Young age (less than 8 years old)

Posteriorly-located headache – if migraine symptoms increase and neuro exam normal, not a tumor

New onset or worsening headache that is not a migraine

Postural headache

Nighttime awakening headache and or vomiting

Early morning headache and or vomiting

Neurocutaneous stigmata

** Note - >95% of children with intracranial mass will have objective neurologic findings

Acute Migraine Management in ED/Inpatient (Pediatrics)

See also: Status Migrainosus Pathway in the NCH Pathways section.

Step / AgentIndication / NotesDose (children/adolescents)Key Cautions / Pearls
Migraine "cocktail"First-line; may repeat q8h if stableIV NS bolus 10–20 mL/kg if dehydrated; prochlorperazine 0.15 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg) or metoclopramide 0.1–0.2 mg/kg IV (max 10 mg); ketorolac 0.5 mg/kg IV (max 30 mg) ± acetaminophen 10–15 mg/kg PO/IV (max 1,000 mg); diphenhydramine 1 mg/kg IV (max 50 mg)Avoid ketorolac with renal risk/other NSAIDs; watch for akathisia/dystonia with antidopaminergics.
IV magnesiumAdjunct for moderate–severe migraine not responding to first-line25–50 mg/kg IV (max 2 g) over 20–30 minHypotension, flushing; consider in older kids/teens with refractory attacks.
IV valproate (VPA)Second-line abortive for refractory migraine15–20 mg/kg IV (max 1,000 mg) over 20–30 minAvoid in pregnancy, significant liver disease, urea-cycle concerns; off-label but commonly used.
Oral VPA bridgeOnly with strong concern for short-term relapse (prolonged attacks, high disability, poor follow-up)>50 kg example: 500 mg PO BID × 3–5 days, then 250 mg PO BID × 3–5 daysPediatric ED data (Rosenthal et al., Neurol Clin Pract 2023) show no reduction in recurrence/ED returns; avoid routine use.
Dihydroergotamine (DHE)Refractory migraine/status after cocktail ± magnesium/VPA and no recent triptan0.5–1 mg IV/IM/SC q8h × up to 2–3 doses (adjust per age/weight and local protocol), with antiemeticContraindicated in significant cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, pregnancy, hemiplegic/basilar migraine, or triptan in past 24 h.
Intranasal ketamineStatus migrainosus when failed/cannot receive DHE, no IV access, or multiple ED visits despite above0.1–0.2 mg/kg/dose IN (max 20 mg/dose), q15 min PRN up to 5 doses (max total 100 mg)Monitor for nasal irritation, nausea, dizziness, transient mood/perceptual change, transient BP/HR rise; use with attending oversight and per Status Migrainosus Pathway.

Migraine Pathophysiology

I

Emergent Headache Evaluation

Thunderclap Headache

Definition: Severe headache reaching maximum intensity within 60 seconds ("worst headache of my life").

Must rule out subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH):

  • Step 1: Non-contrast CT head — sensitivity ~98% within 6 hours of onset, drops to ~93% at 24h and ~50% by day 5
  • Step 2: If CT negative and within 2 weeks of onset → lumbar puncture looking for xanthochromia (send tube 4 for spectrophotometry) and elevated RBCs that do not clear
  • Step 3: If LP also negative → consider CTA or MRA to evaluate for unruptured aneurysm, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST), or reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)

Differential diagnosis for thunderclap headache:

  • Subarachnoid hemorrhage (most dangerous — prevalence ~10-25% of thunderclap presentations)
  • Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST)
  • Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS) — "Call-Fleming syndrome"
  • Pituitary apoplexy
  • Arterial dissection (carotid or vertebral)
  • Hypertensive emergency
  • Primary thunderclap headache (diagnosis of exclusion)

Headache Red Flags — "SNOOP" Mnemonic

S — Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, rash) or Secondary risk factors (immunocompromised, malignancy, coagulopathy)

N — Neurologic signs (papilledema, focal deficits, altered mental status, meningismus)

O — Onset sudden (thunderclap) or recent onset in child < 5 years

O — Other associated features: positional (worse lying down → IIH; worse standing → low-pressure), nocturnal or early morning awakening headaches, triggered by Valsalva/cough/exertion

P — Prior headache history: new headache type, pattern change, progressive worsening, refractory to appropriate treatment

When to Image a Pediatric Headache

Urgent imaging (CT head) indicated if:

  • Thunderclap onset
  • Focal neurologic deficits
  • Papilledema or loss of venous pulsations on fundoscopy
  • Altered mental status or worsening GCS
  • Signs of increased ICP: vomiting (especially AM), personality change, head tilt
  • Post-traumatic with "red flag" features
  • VP shunt concern

MRI brain (outpatient) consider if:

  • Headache always same side
  • Headache wakes from sleep or present on awakening
  • Progressive headache over weeks with new neurologic findings
  • Age < 5 with new recurrent headaches
  • Neurofibromatosis (screen for optic pathway glioma)

IIH Workup Essentials

Opening pressure thresholds (LP in lateral decubitus):

  • Normal: < 20 cmH₂O (children) or < 25 cmH₂O (adolescents/obese)
  • Elevated: ≥ 25 cmH₂O (≥ 28 cmH₂O if obese/sedated)
  • Ensure child is calm, legs extended — crying can double pressure readings
